elements.sent-2411-001: Large-scale networks exist in many fields and play an important role in real-world dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the networks are usually latent and expensive to detect, which becomes the main challenge for many applications and empirical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-003: Several statistical methods were proposed to infer the edges, but the complexity of algorithms makes them hard to apply to large-scale networks.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we proposed a general distributed and parallel computing framework for network reconstruction methods via compressive sensing technical, to make them feasible for inferring the super large networks in practice.
elements.sent-2411-005: Combining with the CALMS, we proposed that those estimators enjoy additional theoretical properties, such as consistency and asymptotic normality, we prove that the approximate estimation utilizing the distributed algorithm can keep the theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-006: Networks are commonly existing in our world, which characterize the interactions between different items in many fields, such as the social networks between people and the trading networks between companies.
elements.sent-2411-007: With the deepening of research into various complex dynamic systems, network data and network-based dynamic processes have increasingly become focal points of academic inquiry.
elements.sent-2411-008: From the perspective of empirical analysis, the network structures commonly influence the changes and evolution of the world profoundly \citep{Dhar2014 , like transportation networks between cities, supply chain networks for international trades, and competition relationships in an evolutionary ultimatum game.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Many scholars regard the known network structures as a treatment, focusing on whether existing network connections exert an influence on other variables, which is commonly referred to as network effects identification.
elements.sent-2411-010: Examples include the impact of transportation networks on economic development \citep{Bramoullé2009 and the influence of social networks on U.S.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Many topics about the effects of economic networks have also been proposed, such as the externalities that arise from demand and cost linkages \citep{AC2007 , the effect of inequality in network centrality \citep{GG2010 , and the social interactions in networks \citep{Santos2006, Santos2008, Choi2015 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In all of these applications, the network structures are assumed to be known, which is necessary for further analysis based on networks.
elements.sent-2411-013: Many scholars proposed the methodologies and models including the known network structures as new components and embed them into traditional models and frameworks, such as the spatial autoregressive model and its extensions \citep{ZouLLT2021, TZY2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Besides, some researchers employ statistical models to characterize the generating process of networks and use the observed network structures to estimate model parameters, such the exponential-family random graph models \citep{Hunter2008 and the stochastic block models \citep{Lei2016, LeiRinaldo .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, all of these researches require the use of known network structures for model fitting, statistical inference, and prediction.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the real world, however, large-scale network structures are often latent and inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-017: The most common reasons are the un-affordable cost of data collection and other practical difficulties such as commercial data privacy and government policy regulations, especially for many empirical studies in economics and finance.
elements.sent-2411-018: Besides, the lack of applicable measurements of network structures and the ambiguity in the interactions between network nodes create many difficulties for network analysis \citep{BF2016 , especially for social and political networks \citep{HeDuan2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Although several experimental designs are proposed to identify the unknown network structures \citep{Fet2009, MG2008, WANG2021 for biochemical topics, they are too expensive and labor-intensive to handle the detection of large-scale networks.}
elements.sent-2411-020: These limitations make the network reconstruction problem an meaningful topic.
elements.sent-2411-021: Network reconstruction aim to infer the latent network structure based only on observed nodal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-022: As the nodal features are commonly treated as the dynamics generated with the fixed but latent networks based on some theoretical models, e.g. the evolutionary games \citep{Wang2011WNKY , the synchronization models \citep{Acebrón2005 and resistor network model \citep{WangL2009 , the network reconstruction is known as a challenging ``inverse problem".}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: To address the network reconstruction problem, several approaches leveraging compressive sensing have been introduced to infer large-scale networks.
elements.sent-2411-024: These group of methods reformulate the network reconstruction task as an optimization problem combining both the latent networks structure and the observed dynamics, and estimate the set of edges by solving the optimization problem with respective to the adjacency matrix of the latent networks \citep{Wang2011, HX2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Although several modifications are proposed with appropriate theoretical properties \citep{Xing2024 , this framework requires a complex transformation of the dynamic data and conduct the optimization algorithms based on a huge transferred dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-026: That is, the complexity and the requirements of memory of these algorithms are sensitive to the size of the networks, which makes it almost infeasible for large-scale networks, such as email networks, protein-protein networks and online social networks.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although some distributed algorithm are proposed in the field of computer science, such as distributed ADMM \citep{Lynchbook1996, Telbook, Matamoros, ZK2014, LiuHYW2023 , they are proposed for independent observations and utilizing the data-splitting for parallel computing, which is not applicable for network data.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Also, the theoretical properties the estimator based on these distributed optimization algorithms are not clearly justified.
elements.sent-2411-029: Motivated by these limitations of existing methods for network reconstruction, we propose a new distributed methods to solve the network reconstruction problem namely Parallel Adaptive Lasso with Multi-directional Signals (PALMS).
elements.sent-2411-030: Practically, PALMS can estimate the latent network structures effectively with well-designed sampling process and parallel computing via the multi-directional penalization.
elements.sent-2411-031: Theoretically, we prove the oracle properties of the distributed estimator PALMS while have the adaptive structures in the sub-optimization of the sub-networks.
elements.sent-2411-032: Besides, PALMS can also be treated as a general distributed framework for most of the network reconstruction methods via compressive sensing to reduce the computing time while achieving similar accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-033: And we show that the theatrical properties of the compressive sensing methods, e.g. consistency and asymptotic normality, will be maintained while using PALMS as a distributed framework.
elements.sent-2411-034: The paper organized as follows: we introduce the background of network reconstruction problem with two classic network dynamic model in Chapter 2.
elements.sent-2411-035: The methodology of PALMS and the theorem are proposed in Chapter 3 and the algorithm of PALMS is proposed in Chapter 4.
elements.sent-2411-036: We show the excellent finite-sample performance of PALMS based on simulations in Chapter 5 and apply our methods to several empirical analysis in Chapter 6.
elements.sent-2411-037: We conclude and list the future topics in Chapter 7.
elements.sent-2411-038: The proofs of the theorem are included in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-039: Evolutionary game theory is a framework used to study the strategic interactions within a population of agents who adjust their behaviors according to evolutionary dynamics, and it is shown to have the power to model complex dynamic system based on networks \citep{TGly2023, Wang2011WNKY, Nowak, Frey .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Evolutionary game theory is widely used in biology, economics, and sociology to model phenomena where agents might not necessarily act rationally \citep{GV2003,MB2007, Hummert2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The Ultimatum Game (UG) in networks is one of the famous evolutionary game model used to study the dynamics of players' strategies and deposits within a network structure.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this setting, participants are represented as nodes in a network, and the edges represent the interaction relationships between pairs of participants.
elements.sent-2411-043: In each round of the UG, one participant of a pair acts as the proposer and another as the responder, they need to decide the fraction to share a given amount of deposits without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-044: Specifically, the proposer will propose a fraction $p \in [0,1]$ for sharing, that is only $1-p$ of the deposit is shared to the responder.
elements.sent-2411-045: And then responder need to decides whether to accept or reject the offer based on a minimum accept fraction $q$.
elements.sent-2411-046: If the proposal is rejected, both of them gain $0$ from this round of games.
elements.sent-2411-047: Intuitively, the positions of the proposer and responder are not equal.
elements.sent-2411-048: Therefore, we consider a fair game process in which, for each round of the game based on each edge of the network, both participants take turns playing the roles of proposer and responder.
elements.sent-2411-049: So for each round of the balanced UG within one pair of participants, there are in total four potential outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-050: Let denote the two paticipants as $i$ and $j$, and their strategy pair as $(p_i, q_i)$ and $(p_j, q_j)$ respectively, where each them contains one-round proposal $p$ and one-round minimum acceptable fraction $q$.
elements.sent-2411-051: And then the four potential outcomes could be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{egePALMSSS} P_{i j}=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} p_{j}+1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq q_{j}, p_{j} \geq q_{i}; 1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq q_{j}, p_{j}<q_{i}; p_{j}, p_{i}<q_{j}, p_{j} \geq q_{i}; 0, p_{i}<q_{j}, p_{j}<q_{i}. \end{array}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{egePALMSSS} P_{i j}=\left\{{lll} p_{j}+1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq q_{j}, p_{j} \geq q_{i}; 1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq q_{j}, p_{j}<q_{i}; p_{j}, p_{i}<q_{j}, p_{j} \geq q_{i}; 0, p_{i}<q_{j}, p_{j}<q_{i}. \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: As all the participants are located in a network as nodes, and each edge represents the balanced two-plays, one node (participant) with degree $d$ will play $d$-round of balanced UG independently with $d$ direct neighbors, and the total income of the node (participant) is the summation of the income in each round of games.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, the dynamics on nodal income and strategy-pair in $t$-th round of game can be concluded in the following formula \begin{equation}\label{UGtotalY222P} Y_i^t = \sum_{j=1}^k a_{ij}P^t_{ij}, ~ i=1,\ldots,k,\end{equation} where $a_{ij}$ is the element of adjacency matrix, which represents the network structure of the UG.
elements.sent-2411-054: As the formula \ref{UGtotalY222P shows, the network structure plays an important role in this complex dynamic system.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The Kuramoto model is a mathematical model that describes the synchronization of coupled oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-056: It is originally used to understand how collective behavior arises from the interactions of individual oscillating elements.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, the model is now particularly known for explaining the transition from incoherence to synchronized behavior in systems such as neuronal networks, power grids, and even flocks of birds.
elements.sent-2411-058: Consider $N$ coupled oscillators, where each oscillator $i$ is characterized by its phase $\theta_i(t)$ and natural frequency $\omega_i$.
elements.sent-2411-059: The dynamics of the Kuramoto model are described by the following set of differential equations: \begin{equation}\label{KM} \frac{d\theta_i}{dt} = \omega_i + \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \sin(\theta_j - \theta_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N,\end{equation} where $a_{ij}$ is from the adjacency matrix representing the coupling relationships between oscillators, and we assume the strength of interaction between the oscillators are identical.
elements.sent-2411-060: The right hand side of equation (\ref{KM ) is the rate of change for each nodes, which is effected by the direct neighbors of the oscillator $a_{ij}$ and the difference in their phase $\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, the network structure also determine the dynamics of the whole systems, when we treat both the phase and it's rate of change as nodal features.
elements.sent-2411-062: In both the evolutionary game and synchronization model, the networks are usually given in previous empirical analysis as a part of experimental design.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, in many real-world dynamic systems the network structures are latent but have significant effects.
elements.sent-2411-064: We address the challenging problem to infer the network structure based only on the observed nodal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-065: We formally introduce the model settings and our methodology in the Section \ref{ModelSetting .}
elements.sent-2411-066: We consider a fixed networks $\bm{A}$ with $N$ nodes, the nodal dynamic $\bm{\Psi}\in\mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$ and nodal response $Y \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 1}$, and independent random errors $\bm{\epsilon \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Then the network dynamics for $t$-th round of network dynamics could be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{tdm} Y^t = ( \bm{A} \circ \bm{\Psi}^t) \bm{1} + \bm{\epsilon}^t\end{equation} where $\circ$ is Hadamard product and $\bm{1 =[1,\cdots,1]'$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that the evolutionary games in networks is a special case of (\ref{tdm ) with $\bm{\Psi ^t = \{P_{ij ^t\}$, and the Kuramoto model in networks is a special case of (\ref{tdm ) with $Y^t = [\frac{\Delta \theta_1^t {h ]$ and $\bm{\Psi ^t = \{ \sin(\theta_i^t - \theta_j^t)\}$, $h$ is sampling interval.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Both the evolutionary games and the Kuramoto model are representative example for network dynamics \citep{Wang2011, HX2015, SD2021,Xing2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Now we consider the distributed algorithm with the estimations based on different sub-networks by randomly grouping the nodes and corresponding edges.
elements.sent-2411-071: Let's denote the indexes of grouped nodes as $\Delta$, then the dynamics related to selected nodes is $Y^\Delta$, the adjacency of sub-network is $\bm{A ^\Delta$, and the potential influential values is $\bm{\Psi ^{\Delta}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-074: Some of the estimators $\tilde{\bm{A }$ enjoy the theoretical properties, such as the consistency and asymptotic normality of ALMS and CALMS.}
elements.sent-2411-075: For distributed algorithm, we aim to decompose the network reconstruction problem into several simpler tasks and jointly consider the estimations based on all the outputs from different computing units.
elements.sent-2411-076: Let's consider the $m$ rounds of node-splitting into $k$ different groups, and the estimations of adjacency matrix based on different groups of sub-networks as $\{\tilde{\bm{A _{(1) ^\Delta,\cdots,\tilde{\bm{A _{(m) ^\Delta \}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then the distributed estimator of latent networks are defined as \begin{equation}\label{AGE} \hat{\bm{A}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{l=1}^m \hat{\bm{A}}_{(l)}\end{equation} Based on the powerful adaptive multi-directional penalization, we proposed our distributed PALMS as \begin{equation}\label{PALMS} \hat{A}_{PALMS} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{l=1}^m \hat{A}_{(l)} \hat{A}_{(l)} = \bigcup_{p,q=1}^k \{\tilde{A}^{\Delta(p,q)}_{(l)}\} \tilde{A}^{\Delta(p,q)}_{(l)} = \arg \min_{A_s} \| Y^{\Delta(p,q)}_{(l)} - (A_s \circ \Psi^{\Delta(p,q)}_{(l)}) 1 \|_F + P_\lambda(A_s) \label{ALMS}\end{equation} where $\Delta$ represents the index for sub-networks and ${(l)}$ stands for the $l$-th splitting of data in the first step of Algorithm \ref{DistributedCALMS , $k$ is the number of groups, $\bigcup$ is the union of the estimations based on sub-networks, $P_\lambda(\bm{A _s)$ is the adaptive multi direction penalty defined as \begin{equation}P_\lambda(\bm{A}) = \lambda \sum_{i,j} w_{ij} \min \{ \left|A_{ij}\right| , \left|A_{ij}-1\right| \}\end{equation} where $w_{ij}$ is the adaptive weights.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The details of the weights and discussions could be found in \citet{Xing2024, Zou2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: We could show that under mild conditions, the aggregate estimator $\hat{\bm{A }$ could keep the theoretical results asymptotically for $\tilde{\bm{A }$, such as the consistency and asymptotically normality.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus, based on the estimator in (\ref{ALMS ), we show the PALMS estimator is consistent and asymptotically normal in Theorem \ref{Con and Theorem \ref{AN .}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Distributed network reconstruction algorithm} \label{DistributedCALMS} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$l= 1,\cdots,m$} \STATE Random split the nodes set of the network into $k$ groups, denote as $\mathcal{G}_1,\cdots,\mathcal{G}_k$ such that $\cup_{i=1}^k\mathcal{G}_i=\mathcal{V}$. \FOR{Parallel compute for $i = 1,\cdots, k$} \STATE extract the corresponding network dynamics related to the sub-networks $\mathcal{G}_i$, denoted the dynamic response as $\bm{Y}^\Delta$ and the nodal dynamics $\bm{\Psi}^Delta$ \STATE estimated the network structures of $\mathcal{G}_i$ via $\tilde{\bm{A}}^{\Delta} (\bm{Y}^\Delta, \bm{\Psi}^\Delta)$ which is the classic estimator in (\ref{overallest}), e.g. ALMS and CALMS. \STATE Calculate the residuals of $R_i = Y^\Delta - \bm{\Delta} \hat{X}^{\Delta}$ and $\|R_i\|_2$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE For any dimension of $X$, calculated the aggregated estimation via (\ref{AGE}) \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-093: The complexity of previous network reconstruction algorithm, e.g. Signal Lasso and CALMS, are beyond $O(rN^3)$ for a latent network with size $N$ and $r$ rounds of dynamics, which can be very large for large-scale networks and long sequences of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-094: Besides, as the popular network reconstruction methods, e.g. Signal Lasso and CALMS, are based on the transformed dataset $\bm{\Phi}\in \mathbb{R ^{Nr\times N^2}$ and update the estimations based on matrix-wise operations, which result in a huge need of the memory space of computing.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Thus, it's almost impossible to solve the classic network reconstruction problem on only one computer for large-scale networks.
elements.sent-2411-096: However, with distributed algorithm proposed, we could decomposed the reconstruction of large-scale networks into several independent easy tasks and perform parallel computing with different computing units, and combine the network reconstruction of a small sub-network with $n$ nodes together to infer the whole networks.

elements.sent-2411-100: In this chapter, we test the effectiveness of proposed distributed algorithm with different networks reconstruction methods.
elements.sent-2411-101: We would like to show the significant uplift in computing speed by combining our distributed algorithm to traditional network reconstruction methods.
elements.sent-2411-102: Specifically, the methods compared in this Chapter are network reconstruction with Lasso \citep{HX2015 , Signal Lasso \citep{SD2021 , ALMS \& CALMS \citep{Xing2024 , and their parallel-version with our proposed distributed framework in (\ref{AGE ) denoted as P-lasso, P-Signal Lasso, PALMS and P-CALMS, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The true network structures are generated based on classic and representative random network models, the Erdős-Rényi (ER) random network model, the Barab\'{a si--Albert (BA) scale-free network.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The ER random network model is one of the most fundamental and representative models for homogeneous networks.
elements.sent-2411-105: In this model, each pair of nodes is connected by an edge with a pre-set probability $p$.
elements.sent-2411-106: This results in a binomial degree distribution where the degree of each node follows the binomial distribution $P(k) = \binom{n-1 {k p^k (1-p)^{n-1-k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: As $n$ becomes large and $p$ is small, the degree distribution tends to follow a Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-108: We can control the density of simulated networks by the pre-set parameter $p$ in the following examples.
elements.sent-2411-109: The BA network model is designed to reflect the growth and preferential attachment seen in many real-world networks, such as the hyper-link networks and social networks.
elements.sent-2411-110: The BA model is one of the representative network models for heterogeneous networks, where the degree distribution follows a power law\citep{ABAB .}
elements.sent-2411-111: That is a small number of nodes have significantly more connections than the majority of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-112: The model starts with an initial set of nodes and, at each time step, a new node is added to the network and connects to existing nodes with a probability proportional to their degree.
elements.sent-2411-113: Networks generated by the BA model are characterized by their scale-free property, which is a feature commonly observed in real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-114: For the dynamics generated based on given network structures, we have three settings to test and compare different network reconstruction methods: the evolutionary ultimatum game dynamics, the multi-normal dynamics and the synchronization model dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-115: The details of the dynamic generating process are described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-116: Data generating process 1: The data are generated based on the evolutionary ultimatum game in networks introduced in previous section.
elements.sent-2411-117: In our evolutionary game model, each player's strategy pair $(p_i, q_i)$ is initially assigned randomly from a standard uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-118: At the end of each round, players have the opportunity to update their strategies based on their payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-119: Specifically, the probability that player $i$ adopts a new strategy is given by $(1 + \exp(\frac{Y_i^r - Y_j^r {N ))^{-1}$, where $Y_i^r$ and $Y_j^r$ are the payoffs of players $i$ and $j$ in round $r$, and $K$ is a constant controlling the selection intensity.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The payoff matrix $P^{(r)}$ for the $r$-th round is computed based on Equation~(\ref{egePALMSSS ).}
elements.sent-2411-121: To express the payoffs of player $i$, we rewrite them in the form: \begin{equation}Y^t = ( \bm{A} \circ \bm{P}^t) \bm{1} + \bm{\epsilon}^t\end{equation} where $Y_i = \left( Y_i^1, Y_i^2, \ldots, Y_i^r \right)^\top$ is the vector of payoffs for player $i$ over $r$ rounds, $X_i = \left( a_{i1 , a_{i2 , \ldots, a_{ik \right)^\top$ represents the connectivity of player $i$ to other players (i.e., the adjacency information), $\bm{\Phi _i^{(2)}$ is a matrix whose $r$-th row corresponds to the payoff coefficients from the payoff matrix $P^{(r)}$ in round $r$, $\epsilon_i$ is a normally distributed random error term following $N(0,1)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: For an evolutionary game involving $N$ players over $r$ rounds, the entire dynamics process can be formulated in matrix form as: \begin{equation}Y = \Phi X + \Xi,\end{equation} where: $Y$ is a stacked vector of all players' payoffs, represented as $ Y = (Y_1', Y_2', \dots, Y_n')' \in \mathbb{R ^{rn \times 1}$, where $Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_n$ are individual payoffs of the players.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Similarly, $X$ is a the vectorization of the adjacency matrix, or stacked vector of all players' connectivity vectors, denoted as $X = vec(\bm{A )$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The matrix $\Phi$ is a block-diagonal matrix composed of the individual payoff coefficient matrices, expressed as $\Phi^{(2) = \operatorname{diag (\bm{P ^1, \bm{P ^2, \dots, \bm{P ^r) \in \mathbb{R ^{rN \times n(N-1)}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, $\bm{\Xi}$ is the vector of random error terms.
elements.sent-2411-126: This formulation captures how each player's payoffs are influenced by their connections with other players and the strategies employed over multiple rounds.
elements.sent-2411-127: Data generating process 2: The loading matrix $\bm{\Phi ^{(1)}$ follows a multivariate normal distribution $N_p(\mu,\Sigma)$ with $\mu_j\sim Unif(-1,1)$, $\Sigma_{jj \sim Unif(1,3)$ and $\Sigma_{ij =0$ for $i\neq j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: We generate the response by $Y_i=\bm{\Phi ^{(1) _iX+\epsilon_i$, where $\epsilon_i \sim N(0,1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Data generating process 3: The data are generated based on a synchronization model introduced in Section \ref{PRE .}
elements.sent-2411-130: We consider the discrete case and fix time step $h=0.01$, and generate the data with the following update formula for the $t$-th step: \begin{equation}\frac{y_i^t}{h}= c \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{i j} \sin \left(\theta_{j}^t-\theta_{i}^t \right),\end{equation} where $c$ is a constant for coupling strength.
elements.sent-2411-131: For $r$ discrete changes of $k$ vertices, we define $\phi_{i j ^t=c \sin \left(\theta_{j ^t-\theta_{i ^t\right)$ and let $Y_i=(y_i^1,\ldots,y_i^r)$ and $Y=(Y_1',\ldots,Y_k')$, $X_i = (a_{i1 ,\ldots,a_{ik )$ and $X=(X_1',\ldots,X_k')$, \begin{equation}\bm{\Phi}^{(3)}_{i}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc} \phi_{i 1}^1 \phi_{i, 2}^1 \cdots \phi_{i_{k}}^1 \phi_{i 1}^2 \phi_{i, 2}^2 \cdots \phi_{i_{k}}^2 \vdots \vdots \vdots \vdots \phi_{i 1}^r \phi_{i, 2}^r \cdots \phi_{i_{k}}^r \end{array}\right)\end{equation} and $\bm{\Phi ^{(3) =diag(\bm{\Phi ^{(3) _{1 ,...,\bm{\Phi ^{(3) _{k )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-133: To compare the contributions of distributed computation in network reconstruction, we have selected the following evaluation metrics to compare the performance of different methods in the simulations:.
elements.sent-2411-134: CPU Time (Time): This metric excludes the time for data pre-processing (i.e., data format conversion) and only considers the CPU time used for completing network reconstruction estimations by each algorithm, measured in seconds.
elements.sent-2411-135: Mean Square Error (MSE) of Network Reconstruction: This metric is used to measure the magnitude of the overall network reconstruction error, calculated using the following formula \begin{equation}MSE(\hat{X})=\frac{\sum_{j=1}^p(X_j-\hat{X}_j)^2}{p}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-136: Success Rates for Nonexistent Links (SRNL): This metric is used to measure the accuracy of the overall network reconstruction for empty edges, calculated by the ratio between the number of correctly predicted nonexistent links and the total number of nonexistent links: \begin{equation}ED_0(\hat{X})=\frac{\sum_{j=1}^p I(X_j=0) I(\hat{X}_j=0)}{\sum_{j=1}^p I(X_j=0)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: Success Rates for Existing Links (SREL): this metric is used to measure the accuracy of the overall network reconstruction for existing edges, calculated by the ratio between the number of correctly predicted links and the total number of links: \begin{equation}ED_1(\hat{X})=\frac{\sum_{j=1}^p I(X_j=1) I(\hat{X}_j=1)}{\sum_{j=1}^p I(X_j=1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-138: For each simulation settings, we repeats for 50 times and report the averages and corresponding standard deviations in the following tables.
elements.sent-2411-139: The details of settings and comparisons are shown in the captions.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Simulation results based on ER network
elements.sent-2411-140: We firstly compare the performance of different network reconstruction methods on the network dynamics generated based on different models and show the results in Table \ref{GDPtable .}
elements.sent-2411-141: It worth nothing that the distributed algorithms PALMS have a significantly shorter computing time in all simulated examples compared to the CALMS based on the whole networks.
elements.sent-2411-142: While achieving the high computational efficiency, the PALMS can achieve a similar accuracy of network reconstructions in terms of the MSE in both simulated examples based on evolutionary games and synchronization models.
elements.sent-2411-143: We check the effectiveness of distributed framework with increasing number of nodes in networks and show the result in Table \ref{Ntable .}
elements.sent-2411-144: As the size of network increases, the computing time of the traditional network reconstruction algorithm, like Signal lasso, ALMS and CALMS, increase largely with only one computing unit.
elements.sent-2411-145: As we analysized in Chapter 3, the complexity of those algorithms are usually larger than $O(N^3)$.
elements.sent-2411-146: When we apply the distributed network reconstruction algorithm, the computing speed increases largely for any given size of the networks.
elements.sent-2411-147: And the CPU times for distributed algorithms are relatively not so sensitive to growing networks.
elements.sent-2411-148: In Table \ref{Densitytable , we check the performance and computing cost for reconstruction algorithms for networks with different expected density.}
elements.sent-2411-149: When the number of connected edges is large, the observed nodal dynamics would become more complex compared to the dynamics generated from a sparse network.
elements.sent-2411-150: Thus, the individual algorithm needs more updates for convergence.
elements.sent-2411-151: Many network reconstruction methods based on ADMM optimization may not converge very quick, which leads to an increase CPU Times for inferring the complex network structures.
elements.sent-2411-152: Consistently with the previous example in Table \ref{Ntable , the distributed network reconstruction algorithm gives a uplift of computing speed largely in every settings.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Besides, the Lasso \citep{HX2015 have almost no power to give the $1$ estimates and the methods based on multi-penalties (Signal lasso, ALMS and CALMS) achieve more accurate estimations for all the cases, which is coincide with the results in previous work \citep{Xing2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: We also investigate the good choice of two parameter $k$ and $m$.
elements.sent-2411-155: As we introduced in Chapter 1, the choice of $k$ is a trade-off between bias and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-156: If we choose a large $k$, the the size of sub-network will be small and the computing time will be shorter by parallel algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-157: And if we choose $k=1$, then the distributed algorithm reduce to the CALMS as there's no sub-sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-158: When $m>1$, there will be some correlation between the estimators for one edge, as their sub-networks may possible included the same dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-159: Although, the asymptotic variance of the aggregated variance will decrease with increasing $m$, large $m$ will uplift the computing cost directly.

elements.sent-2411-170: We test our proposed distributed methods with several real-world structures, including the email-connection network among the scholars in European institutions\footnote{https://snap.stanford.edu/data/email-Eu-core.html , the gene interaction network in biological experiments\footnote{https://snap.stanford.edu/data/S-cerevisiae.html , the protein-protein interaction network\footnote{http://vlado.fmf.uni-lj.si/pub/networks/data/bio/Yeast/Yeast.htm and the Facebook friendship-relationship network \footnote{https://snap.stanford.edu/data/ego-Facebook.html and Amazon product co-purchasing networks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: The mail-networks dataset is a network dataset generated from email communications within a large European research institution.
elements.sent-2411-172: It provides anonymized information on email exchanges between the institution's members and highlights how people are connected through their email communications.
elements.sent-2411-173: This large-scale network have 1005 nodes and 25571 edges and 105461 triangles, which includes both internal and external communication links.
elements.sent-2411-174: The gene network dataset represents a network of gene regulation in the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
elements.sent-2411-175: In this network, each node corresponds to an operon, which is a group of genes transcribed together into a single mRNA molecule.
elements.sent-2411-176: The network includes 690 nodes, 1094 directed edges and 72 triangles.
elements.sent-2411-177: A directed edge from operon $i$ to operon $j$ means that $i$ is regulated by a transcriptional factor encoded by $j$.
elements.sent-2411-178: The protein-protein interaction network in budding yeast dataset \citep{Bu2003 , consists of a network that represents interactions between proteins in Saccharomyces cerevisiae (budding yeast).}
elements.sent-2411-179: The dataset includes 2361 vertices (proteins) and 7182 edges (interactions), with 536 loops.
elements.sent-2411-180: Facebook friendship-relationship network is a collection of anonymity Facebook data that focuses on users' friends lists \citep{Leskovec2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-181: It contains ego networks, which capture the relationships between a user (ego) and their friends (alters), as well as the relationships between those friends.
elements.sent-2411-182: The large network are obtained by merging the ego networks and includes 4039 nodes and 88234 edges.
elements.sent-2411-183: The network has a high average clustering coefficient of 0.6055, indicating that users' friends tend to be friends with each other.

elements.sent-2411-223: Reconstructing large-scale latent networks from observed dynamics is crucial for understanding complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, the existing methods based on compressive sensing are often rendered infeasible in practice by prohibitive computational and memory costs.
elements.sent-2411-225: To address this challenge, we introduce a new distributed computing framework for efficient large-scale network reconstruction with parallel computing, namely PALMS (Parallel Adaptive Lasso with Multi-directional Signals).
elements.sent-2411-226: The core idea of PALMS is to decompose the complex global problem by partitioning network nodes, enabling the parallel estimation of sub-networks across multiple computing units.
elements.sent-2411-227: This strategy substantially reduces the computational complexity and storage requirements of classic methods.
elements.sent-2411-228: By using the adaptive multi-directional regularization on each computing unit, we also establish the consistency of PALMS estimator theoretically.
elements.sent-2411-229: Extensive simulation studies and empirical analyses on several large-scale real-world networks validate the computational efficiency and robust reconstruction accuracy of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-230: Networks are pervasive in our world, characterizing interactions among diverse entities across numerous domains, such as the social networks with corresponding interactions between people and international trade networks with related economic growth.
elements.sent-2411-231: As network data and network-based dynamics have been widely collected \citep{chen2018structural , the analysis of the complex dynamic systems with networks become one of the popular topics in both physics and machine learning \citep{HX2015, SD2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-232: In many empirical conclusions, network structures are found profound to influence global dynamics and evolution of dynamic systems \citep{dhar2014prediction , such as economic networks \citep{amiti2007economic, bramoulle2009identification , social networks \citep{Santos2006, santos2008social, Choi2015 , and competitive interactions in evolutionary ultimatum games \citep{Antinyan2020 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: In the classic network analysis, the network structures are commonly treated as fixed components in statistical model, such as the spatial auto-regressive models \citep{zou2021network, TZY2023 community detection methods \citep{Zhao2012, LeiRinaldo, airoldi2008mixed and the models for identification of network effect \citep{GG2010, Choi2015 and nodal effects \citep{xing2024golfs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Consequently, all such analyses presuppose known network structures as prerequisites for model fitting, statistical inference and prediction.
elements.sent-2411-235: However, the real-world large-scale network structures are usually latent and not accessible due to several reasons, such as budget constrains \citep{xing2025calms , which makes all methods above inapplicable in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Instead of accessing network structures directly, networks reconstruction provides another alternative way to address this issue by inferring the latent networks utilizing the network-based dynamics \citep{HX2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-237: As the observed network-based dynamics are usually noisy and affected by the latent structures, the network reconstruction are also known as a challenging ``inverse problem" \citep{HECKER200986, Timme_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-238: To address the network reconstruction problem, several approaches leveraging compressive sensing have recently been proposed \citep{Wang2011, HX2015, SD2021, adaptivesignallasso .}
elements.sent-2411-239: This group of methods reformulates the network reconstruction task as an optimization problem combining both the latent networks structure and the observed dynamics, and estimates the set of edges by solving the specially-designed optimization problem with respective to the adjacency matrix of the latent networks.
elements.sent-2411-240: Thus, different regularization can be used within this framework to obtain the sparse network estimations, such as the Lasso penalties \citep{HX2015 and Signal Lasso penalties \citep{SD2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Although several modifications based on compressive sensing are more accurate with appropriate theoretical properties \citep{xing2025calms , they all require a complex transformation of the dynamic data and conduct the optimization algorithms based on the special data transformation, which can introduce a very large-scale loading matrix when the size of latent network is large \citep{HX2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Due to the considerable algorithmic complexity and extensive memory requirements that scale with the size of the latent network, compressive-sensing based methods are largely infeasible for large-scale networks, such as online social networks and bio-networks.
elements.sent-2411-243: Motivated by these limitations while inferring the large-scale networks, we propose a new distributed framework to solve the network reconstruction problem, namely Parallel Adaptive Lasso with Multi-directional Signals (PALMS).
elements.sent-2411-244: PALMS decomposes the complex network reconstruction problem into many tasks via splitting of network dynamics, and reconstructs the sub-networks with parallel computing.
elements.sent-2411-245: We prove that the PALMS estimator using specially designed adaptive multi-directional penalization is consistent for latent true networks.
elements.sent-2411-246: Furthermore, we demonstrate that the proposed distributed framework is compatible with a wide range of network reconstruction methods that use compressive sensing.
elements.sent-2411-247: This allows for a significant reduction in computing time while maintaining a comparable level of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-248: The comparisons between PALMS and previous classic methods in network reconstructions are shown in Table \ref{tab:method_comparison .}

elements.sent-2411-256: In the field of network reconstruction, the sequences of nodal features are commonly treated as the dynamics generated with the fixed but latent networks.
elements.sent-2411-257: To better describe the mechanism of network-based dynamics, several representative dynamic models for network-based systems are commonly considered in network reconstruction, such as evolutionary games \citep{Wang2011WNKY and synchronization models \citep{Acebrón2005 .}}

elements.sent-2411-260: Evolutionary game theory provides a framework for studying strategic interactions within populations of agents that adapt their behavior over time.
elements.sent-2411-261: While originally developed for economic studies, the evolutionary game is now widely used in fields such as biology and sociology \citep{Hummert2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-262: The ultimatum game in networks is one of the well-known evolutionary game model for studying the dynamics of players' strategies and deposits within a network structure with exploration within the intricate fabric of large-scale networks, which are used to offer insights into how social structures shape economic and social behaviors and thus widely considered in network reconstruction problem \citep{HX2015, SD2021 as an representative dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-263: In the classic ultimatum game, participants are represented as nodes in a network, and the edges represent the interaction relationships between pairs of participants.
elements.sent-2411-264: In each round of the ultimatum game, one participant of a pair acts as the proposer and another as the responder, they need to decide the fraction to share a given amount of deposits without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-265: And then responder need to decides whether to accept or reject the offer based on a pre-set minimum accept fraction $q$.
elements.sent-2411-266: A fair game process in considered in network-based ultimatum game, where both participants take turns playing the roles of proposer and responder for each round of the game.
elements.sent-2411-267: Then for each round of the balanced ultimatum game within one pair of participants, there are four potential outcomes summarized in the following \eqref{egePALMSSS \begin{equation}\label{egePALMSSS} P_{i j}=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} p_{j}+1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq r_{j}, p_{j} \geq r_{i}; 1-p_{i}, p_{i} \geq r_{j}, p_{j}<r_{i}; p_{j}, p_{i}<r_{j}, p_{j} \geq r_{i}; 0, p_{i}<r_{j}, p_{j}<r_{i}. \end{array}\right.\end{equation}.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Distributed Network Reconstruction Algorithm} \label{DistributedCALMS} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$l= 1, \cdots, m$} \STATE Randomly partition the network's node set $\mathcal{V}$ into $k$ groups, denoted as $\mathcal{G}_1, \cdots, \mathcal{G}_k$, such that $\cup_{i=1}^k\mathcal{G}_i=\mathcal{V}$; \FOR{$k_1, k_2 = 1, \cdots, k$ in parallel} \STATE Extract sub-networks $\mathcal{G}_{k_1}$ and $\mathcal{G}_{k_2}$ and their associated network dynamics; transmit to a sub-computational node; \STATE Denote the node response as $\bm{Y}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}$ and transform the node dynamics $\bm{\Psi}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}$ into the design matrix $\bm{\Phi}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}$ required by the compressed sensing-based algorithm; \STATE Estimate the sub-network structure by solving the optimization problem \eqref{overallest}, denoting the estimate as $\tilde{\bm{A}}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}_{(l)}$; \STATE Calculate the residuals $\mathbf{R}_{(l)} = \bm{Y}_{(l)}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}} - \text{vec}(\tilde{\bm{A}}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}_{(l)})\bm{\Phi}^{\mathcal{N}_{k_1k_2}}$; \ENDFOR \STATE Obtain the integrated estimate for the entire network, $\hat{\bm{A}}_{(l)}$, according to \eqref{PALMS_zhenghe}. \ENDFOR \STATE Compute the final aggregated estimate using the formula in (\ref{PALMS}). \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-334: To compare the contributions of distributed computation in network reconstruction, we refer to the classic work in network reconstruction and compare the performance of different methods in the simulations with following 4 measures: (1) CPU Time: CPU time used for completing network reconstruction estimations by each algorithm in seconds; (2) Mean Square Error (MSE): This metric is used to measure the magnitude of the overall network reconstruction error, calculated using the following formula $\text{MSE (\hat{X )=\sum_{j=1 ^p(X_j-\hat{X _j)^2/p$; (3) Success Rates for Nonexistent Links (SRNL): This metric is used to measure the accuracy of the overall network reconstruction for empty edges, calculated by the ratio between the number of correctly predicted nonexistent links and the total number of nonexistent links: $\text{SRNL (\hat{X )= \sum_{j=1 ^p \mathrm{I (X_j=0) \mathrm{I (\hat{X _j=0)/\sum_{j=1 ^p \mathrm{I (X_j=0)$; (4) Success Rates for Existing Links (SREL): this metric is used to measure the accuracy of the overall network reconstruction for existing edges, calculated by the ratio between the number of correctly predicted links and the total number of links $\text{SREL (\hat{X )= \sum_{j=1 ^p \mathrm{I (X_j=1) \mathrm{I (\hat{X _j=1)/\sum_{j=1 ^p \mathrm{I (X_j=1).$ For each simulation settings, we repeats for $500$ times and report the averages and corresponding standard deviations of $4$ measures in tables.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-358: For the reconstruction problem of the large-scale and sparse network in our empirical analysis, PALMS achieves the dominant performance in detecting the exist connections in large-scale network reconstructions in term of the SREL.
elements.sent-2411-359: Although MSE of Lasso methods are very small, SREL gives a straightforward measure of the ability to estimate the latent connections in large-scale and sparse networks, as the MSE can also be extremely small when an empty network is estimated for sparse networks with few edges.

elements.sent-2411-361: While several previously proposed methods are no longer feasible for inferring the latent network structures, the proposed distributed algorithm can reduce the computing complexity and requirements of memory, which can still handle the reconstruction task for large-scale network in practice with accurate estimation.
elements.sent-2411-362: This paper introduced a distributed computing framework to effectively overcome the computational barriers in large-scale network reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-363: By partitioning the network and employing parallel processing, PALMS makes computationally-intensive compressive sensing techniques feasible for large networks by drastically reducing execution time.
elements.sent-2411-364: We theoretically proved that PALMS estimator enjoys the appropriate statistical property consistency for the latent true network structure.
elements.sent-2411-365: The superior performance of PALMS was demonstrated in both extensive simulations and real-world data analysis, and the R tools for PALMS are publicly available online\footnote{https://github.com/ZhaoyuXingStat/PALMS .}
elements.sent-2411-366: Although our method achieves high accuracy with significantly less computation time, future work could investigate the impact of different network partitioning strategies and the design to a robust network reconstruction methods.
